title: Ora Hashimoto — Founder & CEO, Prism Protocol
compiled_truth:
Ora Hashimoto is the founder and CEO of [Prism Protocol](companies/prism-protocol), a developer tools company she started in late 2025 after spending nearly a decade bouncing between infrastructure and devtools startups. She's known in the ecosystem for her unusual combination of deep technical chops and an almost obsessive focus on product craft — the kind of person who will rewrite an SDK three times before shipping if the ergonomics don't feel right.

Before Prism, Ora held engineering and engineering-leadership roles at two mid-stage infrastructure companies, neither of which survived past Series B. She's talked publicly about how those experiences shaped her thinking: she saw firsthand how technically impressive systems can fail if they ignore the developer experience layer. That conviction became the seed for [Prism Protocol](companies/prism-protocol), which builds tooling that sits between raw infrastructure primitives and the application layer, making it easier for teams to integrate complex data pipelines without writing bespoke glue code.

Hashimoto tends to stay out of the conference circuit, preferring small-group technical discussions and design reviews over keynotes. That said, she's shown up at a handful of invite-only gatherings in 2027 and 2028, usually when the topic touches deterministic systems or single-pass architectures — areas where Prism has been quietly building integrations. People who've worked with her describe meetings that are part whiteboard session, part interrogation; she asks a lot of pointed questions and doesn't let vague answers slide.

Ora is product-obsessed in a way that sometimes frustrates her own team. There are stories about her blocking launches over minor inconsistencies in error messages or CLI output formatting. But the result is a developer tool that people genuinely enjoy using, which has helped Prism punch above its weight in adoption metrics relative to headcount. She's also known for maintaining a personal "friction log" — a running document of every rough edge she encounters in her own product, updated dailey.

She's based in the Pacific Northwest, runs a small but tight engineering team, and has been selective about fundraising, preferring to stay lean. Her philosophy is that developer tools should be opinionated but composable, and that conviction permeates everything Prism ships.
timeline:
- **2025-11-10** | Ora Hashimoto incorporates [Prism Protocol](companies/prism-protocol) and begins building initial prototype with two co-engineers.
- **2026-03-22** | Prism Protocol ships its first public beta — a CLI-based pipeline integration tool aimed at infrastructure teams.
- **2026-07-15** | Ora closes a small pre-seed round; terms undisclosed but described by her as "enough to hire four more people and keep the lights on."
- **2026-11-08** | Hashimoto presents at a private developer infrastructure roundtable in Portland, OR. First public mention of interest in single-pass data primitives.
- **2027-02-14** | Prism Protocol v1.0 ships with support for deterministic pipeline configurations. Adoption spikes among early-stage infra teams.
- **2027-06-03** | Ora attends a small working session with the Anchor Codec team to discuss potential integration pathways between Prism's tooling and Anchor's primitves.
- **2027-09-19** | Prism Protocol announces a seed round, brings team to 12 engineers.
- **2027-12-01** | Hashimoto publishes a widely-shared blog post titled "Glue Code Is Technical Debt" arguing for opinionated developer tools over flexible-but-fragile abstractions.
- **2028-04-11** | [Prism Protocol](companies/prism-protocol) ships an experimental integration layer for deterministic data infrastructure workflows. Ora demos it at a private event in Seattle.
- **2028-08-22** | Ora Hashimoto joins the technical advisory group for an emerging standards effort around single-pass data primitives interoperability.

_facts.name: Ora Hashimoto
_facts.title: Founder & CEO, Prism Protocol
_facts.background: Founded Prism Protocol in 2025; previously at infrastructure and developer-tools companies
